The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]]

The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]]

The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takeda Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takeda Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takeda Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takeda Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[en:Takeda Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[arz:ضريح تاكيدا]] {{Commons category|Takeda-jinja}} [[de:Takeda-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Τακέντα]] [[eu:Takeda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد تاکدا]] [[fr:Takeda-jinja]] [[id:Kuil Takeda]] [[ja:武田神社]] [[pt:Takeda Jinja]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าทาเกดะ]] [[zh:武田神社]]

The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[arz:ضريح تاكيدا]] {{Commons category|Takeda-jinja}} [[de:Takeda-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Τακέντα]] [[eu:Takeda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد تاکدا]] [[fr:Takeda-jinja]] [[id:Kuil Takeda]] [[ja:武田神社]] [[pt:Takeda Jinja]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าทาเกดะ]] [[zh:武田神社]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[:en:Death anniversary|Death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[arz:ضريح تاكيدا]] {{Commons category|Takeda-jinja}} [[de:Takeda-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Τακέντα]] [[eu:Takeda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد تاکدا]] [[fr:Takeda-jinja]] [[id:Kuil Takeda]] [[ja:武田神社]] [[pt:Takeda Jinja]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าทาเกดะ]] [[zh:武田神社]]

The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan) | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[arz:ضريح تاكيدا]] {{Commons category|Takeda-jinja}} [[de:Takeda-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Τακέντα]] [[eu:Takeda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد تاکدا]] [[fr:Takeda-jinja]] [[id:Kuil Takeda]] [[ja:武田神社]] [[pt:Takeda Jinja]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าทาเกดะ]] [[zh:武田神社]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Yamanashi Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takeda Shrine
武田神社 | image = KofuTakedaJinja3.jpg | caption = Takeda Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|12.75|N|138|34|38.9|E|region:JP-19_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_type = Japan Yamanashi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Takeda Shingen]] | founded_by = | established = 1919 | date_destroyed = | location = 2611 Kofuchū-machi, Kōfu-shi, Yamanashi-ken | website = {{URL|http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Takeda Shrine'''|武田神社|Takeda Jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Kōfu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' of [[Takeda Shingen]]. The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[:en:Death anniversary|Death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan) | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[arz:ضريح تاكيدا]] {{Commons category|Takeda-jinja}} [[de:Takeda-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Τακέντα]] [[eu:Takeda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد تاکدا]] [[fr:Takeda-jinja]] [[id:Kuil Takeda]] [[ja:武田神社]] [[pt:Takeda Jinja]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าทาเกดะ]] [[zh:武田神社]]

The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[:en:Lady Sanjō|Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan) | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[arz:ضريح تاكيدا]] {{Commons category|Takeda-jinja}} [[de:Takeda-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Τακέντα]] [[eu:Takeda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد تاکدا]] [[fr:Takeda-jinja]] [[id:Kuil Takeda]] [[ja:武田神社]] [[pt:Takeda Jinja]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าทาเกดะ]] [[zh:武田神社]]

The shrine's annual celebration is on April 12, Shingen's [[:en:Death anniversary|Death anniversary]]. ==History== Following the defeat of the [[Takeda clan]] during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the [[Tsutsujigasaki Castle|Tsutsujigasaki]] fortified residence of [[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] was allowed to fall into ruins, and the center of Kōfu shifted south to surround [[Maizuru Castle Park|Kōfu Castle]], the center of administrative power under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Tsutsujigasaki ruins came under government protection for their historic value and were eventually made a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Monument of Japan]].{{cite web|url=http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/138294 |title=武田氏館跡 |website=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 September 2018}} Following the visit of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] to Yamanashi Prefecture in 1880, a local movement developed for a shrine to honor the loyalists who had served in the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. This dovetailed with the [[State Shintō]] projects to erect shrines dedicated to historic figures noted for their martial prowess and with the need for a shrine to honor the war dead of the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]]. In 1915, [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] commissioned the shrine, which was completed in 1919. The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] before World War II. The shrine grounds contain a museum, in which numerous artifacts pertaining to Takeda Shingen, including armor, weapons, battle standards, and personal effects, are displayed. ==Cultural properties== ===National Important Cultural Property=== The Takeda Shrine owns a ''[[tachi]]'' [[Japanese sword]] presented to Yamanashi Prefecture by [[Sanjō Sanetomi]] to commemorate the visit by Emperor Meiji in 1880. The sword had passed into the [[Sanjō clan]] as part of the bridal gifts from the Takeda clan when [[:en:Lady Sanjō|Lady Sanjō]] married Takeda Shingen, and dates from the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]. It has a length of 64.5 cm and a width of 2.9 cm. The sword is attributed to the noted swordsmith Yoshioka Ichimonji from [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen]], although its half has only the ''[[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]'' for "ichi" graved upon it. The sword is a [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan) | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح تاكيدا]] {{Commons category|Takeda-jinja}} [[de:Takeda-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Τακέντα]] [[eu:Takeda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد تاکدا]] [[fr:Takeda-jinja]] [[id:Kuil Takeda]] [[ja:武田神社]] [[pt:Takeda Jinja]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าทาเกดะ]] [[zh:武田神社]]

The sword is a [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan) | National Important Cultural Property]].{{cite web |url=https://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |title=山梨の文化財ガイド（データベース）工芸品01 |language=Japanese |publisher=Yamanashi Prefecture |accessdate= |archive-date=2013-06-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130602212113/http://www.pref.yamanashi.jp/gakujutu/bunkazaihogo/bunkazai_data/yamanashinobunkazai_kougeihin001.html#k0002ll |url-status=dead }} ==See also== *[[:en:Takeda Shingen|Takeda Shingen]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat|Takeda-jinja}} *[http://www.takedajinja.or.jp/ Official site] (Japanese) *[http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php Kōfu city official sightseeing guide] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211109150525/http://en.kofushingen.jp/tourism/t01.php |date=2021-11-09 }} *[https://www.yamanashi-kankou.jp/foreign/english/spot/006.html Yamanashi Prefecture official sightseeing guide] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Kōfu, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Takeda clan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[file: KofuTakedaJinja1.jpg|thumb|right|Main gate to Takeda Shrine]] [[sn:Takeda Shrine]] [[en:Takeda Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[arz:ضريح تاكيدا]] {{Commons category|Takeda-jinja}} [[de:Takeda-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Τακέντα]] [[eu:Takeda santutegia]] [[fa:معبد تاکدا]] [[fr:Takeda-jinja]] [[id:Kuil Takeda]] [[ja:武田神社]] [[pt:Takeda Jinja]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าทาเกดะ]] [[zh:武田神社]]
